title: Marketing planning
summary: A marketing plan turns a vague ambition to 'sell more' into a targeted, evidence-based strategy. This lesson works through the whole of syllabus 4.2: the elements of a marketing plan; market segmentation and its four bases — demographic, geographic, psychographic and behavioural; targeting and the choice of an appropriate target market; market positioning and how to read and build a position (perception) map; product differentiation and the unique selling point (USP); and why a marketing plan matters in the first place. Every worked example is set out exactly the way our marking engine awards Business Management marks — AO1 knowledge, AO2 application to context and AO3 analysis and evaluation — so you can see how a supported judgement, not a memorised list, is what earns the top band.
learningObjectives: Identify the key elements of a marketing plan and explain the importance of marketing planning to a business.
Distinguish between the four bases of market segmentation — demographic, geographic, psychographic and behavioural — and apply them to a given business context.
Explain targeting and justify the choice of an appropriate target market for a business.
Explain market positioning and interpret and construct a position (perception) map to show a brand relative to its competitors.
Explain product differentiation and the unique selling point (USP), and evaluate marketing decisions ending in a supported judgement.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Aiming before you fire
simpleExplanation.summary: A marketing plan is the difference between shouting at a crowd and speaking to the right person. Instead of trying to sell one product to everyone, a business splits the market into groups (segmentation), picks the group it can serve best (targeting), and then works to own a clear, desirable spot in that group's mind (positioning). A unique selling point is the single reason that spot belongs to this business and not a rival.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of the whole market as a huge, noisy party. Mass marketing is standing in the doorway yelling the same message at every guest and hoping a few turn around. Marketing planning is smarter: you look around and notice the party is really several smaller groups — students dancing, foodies by the buffet, quiet talkers on the sofa (segmentation). You decide the foodies are the people you can genuinely impress (targeting). Then you walk over with the one thing they can't get anywhere else in the room — your homemade dumplings (your USP) — so that whenever they think 'best food here', they think of you (positioning). A position map is simply a photo of where everyone at the party is standing, so you can see the empty corner nobody else has taken.
steps: Segment the market: divide the broad market into smaller groups that share characteristics, using demographic, geographic, psychographic or behavioural bases.
Choose a target market: assess each segment for size, growth, competition and fit, and select the one(s) the business can serve profitably.
Position the offering: decide how you want the target segment to perceive the product relative to rivals, and map that position against competitors.
Differentiate with a USP: build in and communicate the one meaningful difference — quality, price, ethics, features — that makes the target choose you, then wrap the whole plan around it.

flashcards.back: A document setting out a business's marketing objectives and the strategies and tactics to achieve them. It typically includes analysis of the market, marketing objectives, the target market, positioning and the marketing mix, plus a budget and controls to monitor progress.

Dividing a broad market into smaller sub-groups (segments) of consumers who share characteristics, so the business can serve each group more precisely. Trap: segmentation divides the MARKET, not the product line.
Dividing the market by measurable population characteristics such as age, gender, income, occupation, family size, religion or ethnicity. The most common and easily measured basis.
Dividing the market by location — country, region, climate, urban vs rural, or population density — because needs and tastes often vary by where people live.
Dividing the market by lifestyle, personality, values, attitudes and interests (for example 'health-conscious' or 'status-seeking' consumers), rather than by who people are on paper.
Dividing the market by how consumers act towards a product — usage rate, brand loyalty, benefits sought, or the occasion of purchase (e.g. gifts, seasonal buyers, heavy vs light users).
The specific segment or segments a business selects to aim its marketing mix at, after deciding which groups it can serve most effectively and profitably.

The process of evaluating each market segment (size, growth, competition, fit with the firm's strengths) and choosing which one(s) to serve. Approaches range from mass (undifferentiated) to niche (concentrated) marketing.

Creating a clear, distinctive and desirable image for a product in the minds of the target market relative to competitors. It is about how customers PERCEIVE the brand, not just what it objectively is.
A two-axis diagram plotting brands against two key attributes valued by customers (e.g. price vs quality) to visualise how a product sits relative to rivals and to reveal gaps in the market.
Making a product distinct from competitors' offerings through real or perceived differences — features, design, quality, branding, service or ethics — so customers have a reason to choose it.

The one meaningful feature or benefit that sets a product apart from every rival — lowest price, highest quality, a first-of-its-kind feature, or an ethical stance — and that gives the target customer a clear reason to buy it.

flashcards.front: Trap: segmentation vs targeting vs positioning
flashcards.back: Segmentation DIVIDES the market into groups; targeting CHOOSES which group(s) to serve; positioning SHAPES how the chosen group perceives the product. They are three sequential steps, not synonyms.

flashcards.front: Trap: USP is not a slogan
flashcards.back: A USP is the actual distinctive feature/benefit (e.g. Volvo's safety engineering). A slogan is the catchy phrase used to communicate it. A USP must be meaningful to the target customer, not just 'different'.

A position map shows PERCEPTIONS on two chosen attributes, not sales or market share. An empty space on the map may be a genuine gap in the market — or a space empty because no customers want a product there.

quickCheck.prompt: A gym divides its customers into 'busy professionals who want quick lunchtime workouts' and 'retired members who value social classes'. Which basis of segmentation is this?
options: Demographic segmentation, because it mentions professionals and retirees
Behavioural and psychographic segmentation, because it groups by lifestyle and benefits sought
Geographic segmentation, because gyms are location-based
It is not segmentation at all, it is positioning
quickCheck.answer: Behavioural and psychographic segmentation, because it groups by lifestyle and benefits sought

quickCheck.prompt: Which sequence correctly describes the STP process?
options: Position the product, then target a segment, then divide the market
Divide the market into segments, choose which segment(s) to serve, then shape how they perceive the product
Choose a target market, then create a USP, then segment
Segment, position, then differentiate — targeting is not part of STP
quickCheck.answer: Divide the market into segments, choose which segment(s) to serve, then shape how they perceive the product

quickCheck.prompt: What does a unique selling point (USP) actually refer to?
options: A catchy advertising slogan used in a campaign
The lowest price a business is willing to accept for a product
The one meaningful feature or benefit that distinguishes a product from all rivals and gives the target a reason to buy
The total number of market segments a business serves
quickCheck.answer: The one meaningful feature or benefit that distinguishes a product from all rivals and gives the target a reason to buy

quickCheck.prompt: On a position (perception) map with axes 'low price–high price' and 'basic–premium quality', an empty corner shows:
options: Guaranteed profit if the business moves there
A perceptual space where no current brand is positioned, which may be a gap in the market — or a space customers do not want
The brand with the highest sales
The business's total market share
quickCheck.answer: A perceptual space where no current brand is positioned, which may be a gap in the market — or a space customers do not want

quickCheck.prompt: A student writes: 'The business should segment its market by selling a cheaper version and a premium version of the product.' Why is this wrong?
options: Businesses are not allowed to sell two versions of a product
It confuses segmentation (dividing the MARKET into customer groups) with differentiating the product line
Cheaper versions always reduce profit, so it can never work
Segmentation can only ever be demographic
quickCheck.answer: It confuses segmentation (dividing the MARKET into customer groups) with differentiating the product line

faq.q: What is the difference between segmentation, targeting and positioning?
faq.a: They are three sequential steps, not three names for the same thing. Segmentation divides the whole market into groups of customers who share characteristics. Targeting evaluates those groups and chooses which one(s) the business will actually serve. Positioning then shapes how that chosen group perceives the product relative to competitors. A common exam error is to describe segmentation but call it targeting, or to jump straight to positioning without showing the segment it is aimed at.

faq.q: How many bases of segmentation do I need to know for 4.2?
faq.a: Four: demographic (age, gender, income, occupation), geographic (region, climate, urban/rural), psychographic (lifestyle, values, personality) and behavioural (usage rate, loyalty, benefits sought, occasion). Strong answers do not just list them — they pick the bases that fit the specific business and explain why. A luxury watchmaker, for instance, leans on income (demographic) and status-seeking lifestyle (psychographic); a supermarket meal-deal leans on behavioural (lunchtime occasion) and geographic (near offices).

faq.q: How is a USP different from a slogan or a brand?
faq.a: A USP is the real, distinctive feature or benefit that makes a product genuinely different and better for the target — Volvo's safety engineering, or a bottle made from recycled ocean plastic. A slogan is the short phrase used to communicate it, and the brand is the overall identity that carries it. The marks come from identifying a USP that is meaningful to the target customer, not merely 'different'. A difference nobody values is not a USP.

faq.q: What does an empty space on a position map really tell me?
faq.a: A position map plots perceptions on two attributes (say price and quality), not sales. An empty space means no current brand is perceived to sit there. That MIGHT be a gap in the market worth entering — but it might be empty because customers do not want a product with that combination (for example, high price and low quality). So a gap on the map is a hypothesis to test with research, not an automatic opportunity.

faq.q: Why does a business bother writing a marketing plan at all?
faq.a: A marketing plan forces the business to base decisions on evidence, coordinates the marketing mix so the elements reinforce each other, allocates a budget efficiently, and sets targets it can monitor and control. Without one, spending becomes scattered and reactive. But a plan is only as good as the research behind it and the flexibility to adapt — a rigid plan in a fast-changing market can become a liability, which is exactly the kind of two-sided point that earns evaluation marks.

sections.content: A business that tries to sell one product to 'everyone' usually ends up appealing strongly to no one. Marketing planning fixes that. This lesson covers the whole of syllabus 4.2 — the elements of a marketing plan and why it matters; market segmentation and its four bases (demographic, geographic, psychographic and behavioural); targeting and the choice of an appropriate target market; market positioning and how to read and build a position (perception) map; and product differentiation through a unique selling point (USP). Because 4.2 is assessed with command terms that reach into evaluation, the worked examples are set out the way our marking engine actually awards marks, so you can see why an answer that APPLIES a concept to a business and ends in a SUPPORTED JUDGEMENT outscores a memorised list every time.
What a marketing plan is, and why it matters

A marketing plan is a document that sets out a business's marketing objectives and the strategies and tactics it will use to reach them. It is where research about customers and competitors is turned into decisions. A typical plan analyses the market and the competition, states clear marketing objectives (for example, a target market share or sales growth), identifies the target market and desired positioning, specifies the marketing mix that will deliver it, and attaches a budget and a set of controls to monitor whether the plan is working. The importance of the plan is not that it guarantees success — it does not — but that it makes marketing deliberate rather than accidental.

items: **Elements of a marketing plan:** situation/market analysis, marketing objectives, target market, positioning, the marketing mix (the tactics), a budget, and controls to review performance.
**Coordination:** a plan aligns the elements of the mix so they reinforce one another and support the chosen positioning, rather than pulling in different directions.
**Efficiency and focus:** it allocates a limited budget to the segments and channels most likely to pay off, reducing wasted spending.
**Direction and control:** clear objectives give something to measure against, so the business can spot underperformance early and adapt.
**Limitation to remember:** a plan is only as good as the research behind it, and an inflexible plan can become a liability in a fast-changing market — a point worth raising in evaluation.

sections.content: Market segmentation and its four bases

Market segmentation is the process of dividing a broad market into smaller sub-groups of consumers (segments) who share characteristics, so the business can tailor its offer to each group instead of averaging across everyone. The syllabus expects command of four bases of segmentation. Strong answers do not simply list them — they choose the bases that fit the specific business and justify the choice, because that is where application marks live.

items: **Demographic:** dividing by measurable population traits — age, gender, income, occupation, family size, religion or ethnicity. The most common and easily measured basis (e.g. a toy aimed at 3–6 year-olds).
**Geographic:** dividing by location — country, region, climate, urban vs rural or population density — because needs and tastes vary by where people live (e.g. heavier coats sold in colder regions).
**Psychographic:** dividing by lifestyle, values, personality, attitudes and interests, rather than by who people are on paper (e.g. 'health-conscious' or 'environmentally conscious' consumers).
**Behavioural:** dividing by how consumers act — usage rate, brand loyalty, benefits sought or purchase occasion (e.g. heavy vs light users, or people buying gifts at a particular season).

**Why it helps:** segmentation lets a business match products, messages and prices to a group's real needs, target promotion efficiently and spot under-served gaps — but over-segmenting into tiny groups can raise costs and complexity.

sections.content: Targeting and the choice of target market

Segmentation only describes the market; targeting is where the business decides what to do about it. Targeting means evaluating each segment against criteria such as its size, its growth potential, the strength of competition already there, and how well it fits the firm's own strengths — and then choosing which segment(s) to serve. A mass (undifferentiated) approach aims one offer at the whole market to win volume and economies of scale; a niche (concentrated) approach focuses on one small, well-defined segment to avoid head-on competition and command a premium; a differentiated approach serves several segments each with its own tailored mix. The right choice depends on the business's resources, its product and the market, and justifying that choice for a specific firm is exactly what evaluation questions reward.

items: **Assess each segment:** size and profitability, growth prospects, intensity of competition, and fit with the firm's capabilities and brand.
**Mass/undifferentiated targeting:** one mix for the whole market — low unit costs and high potential volume, but heavy competition and a message that may satisfy no one deeply.
**Niche/concentrated targeting:** one small segment — less competition, strong loyalty and premium pricing, but limited size and vulnerability if that segment's tastes shift.
**Differentiated targeting:** several segments each with a tailored mix — broad reach, but higher costs and complexity.
**Choosing the target market:** commit to the segment the business can serve profitably AND better than rivals, and say why that segment beats the alternatives for THIS firm.

sections.content: Market positioning and position (perception) maps

Once a target segment is chosen, positioning decides how the business wants that segment to perceive its product relative to competitors. Positioning is about perception: a brand is 'positioned' as, say, affordable-and-cheerful, or premium-and-exclusive, or ethical-and-sustainable. A position map (also called a perception map) is the tool used to visualise this. It plots competing brands on two axes representing attributes customers care about — commonly price against quality, but it could be traditional against modern, or basic against luxurious. Reading the map shows where the business sits relative to rivals and, crucially, whether there is an unoccupied space that could signal a gap in the market. But an empty space is a hypothesis, not a promise: it may be empty because customers genuinely do not want that combination.

items: **Positioning:** creating a clear, distinctive and desirable image in the target's mind relative to competitors — it is about perception, not just objective features.
**Position (perception) map:** two axes of attributes customers value (e.g. price vs quality); each brand is plotted as a point to show how it is perceived.
**Spotting gaps:** an empty region may reveal an under-served position — but confirm with research, because 'high price / low quality' is empty for a reason.
**Repositioning:** a business can deliberately shift its perceived position over time (through the mix and branding), though this is costly and slow.
**What the map is NOT:** it shows perceptions, not sales or market share — do not read the axes as revenue.

sections.content: Product differentiation and the unique selling point (USP)

Positioning is only credible if the product is genuinely, or at least perceptibly, different — that is product differentiation. A business differentiates through real or perceived differences in features, design, quality, branding, customer service or ethics. The sharpest expression of differentiation is the unique selling point (USP): the single meaningful feature or benefit that no rival can match and that gives the target customer a clear reason to buy. A USP might be the lowest price, the highest quality, a first-of-its-kind feature, or an ethical stance such as recycled materials. The test of a USP is not merely that it is different, but that the difference matters to the target segment — a feature nobody values is not a USP, however unique it is.

items: **Product differentiation:** distinguishing the product from rivals through features, design, quality, brand, service or ethics — real or perceived.
**Unique selling point (USP):** the one meaningful difference that gives the target a reason to choose this product over every alternative.
**A USP must be meaningful:** it has to matter to the target segment, not just be unusual; a difference customers do not value earns no loyalty.
**USP vs slogan:** the USP is the real feature/benefit; the slogan is the phrase used to communicate it. Do not confuse the two.
**Link to the plan:** the USP anchors positioning and the whole marketing mix, so that price, promotion, product and place all reinforce the same distinctive message.

sections.content: When a question mentions segmentation, targeting or positioning, name the concept precisely and keep the three separate: segmentation DIVIDES the market, targeting CHOOSES a segment, positioning SHAPES perception. Then apply it to the named business — which base fits, which segment, which perceived position — because the application marks (AO2) are won in that link, not in the definition.
Worked example 1 — a command-term evaluation, marked our way

Business Management 4.2 is assessed against three objectives: AO1 rewards relevant knowledge and understanding, AO2 rewards applying that knowledge to the specific business in the stimulus, and AO3 rewards analysis and a balanced evaluation. In the analytic/points scheme each distinct valid point earns credit, but the higher 'examine' and 'evaluate' marks are reserved for answers that combine APPLICATION to context with a BALANCED evaluation that ends in a SUPPORTED JUDGEMENT. Watch how the marks below attach to applied, two-sided reasoning and a justified conclusion — never to a generic list.

sections.question: ‘FreshBox’ is a start-up that delivers recipe kits. It currently markets a single kit to 'everyone who cooks'. It is considering segmenting its market instead. Examine the benefits to a business of segmenting its market. [10]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
Segmenting its market would let FreshBox divide the broad population of home cooks into distinct groups — for example, busy young professionals wanting quick 15-minute meals, families needing larger portions, and health-conscious customers seeking calorie-controlled kits. The first and clearest benefit is more targeted marketing. Instead of one bland message aimed at 'everyone who cooks', FreshBox could craft a specific offer and promotion for each segment — advertising the 15-minute kits on commuter apps, and the family kits through parenting channels. Because the message speaks directly to a group's real needs, response rates and conversion tend to be higher and promotional spending is less wasted.

A second benefit is a better fit between product and customer, which supports differentiation and pricing. By designing kits around a defined segment — say, premium organic ingredients for the health-conscious group — FreshBox can build a clearer USP and justify a higher price to customers who value that benefit, rather than competing only on price for a generic kit. This tends to raise both sales volume within the segment and the margin earned on each sale, and it can build stronger loyalty because customers feel the product was made for them.

Segmentation can also reveal under-served gaps. Analysing the segments might show that no major rival serves, say, single-person low-waste portions — a position FreshBox could occupy with little direct competition.

However, the benefits are not automatic and come with costs. Serving several segments with tailored kits, packaging and campaigns raises production and marketing complexity and cost, which is heavy for a start-up with limited resources. Over-segmenting into very small groups can leave each too small to be profitable. There is also a risk of getting the segmentation wrong if it is based on weak research, so that FreshBox targets a group that does not actually behave as assumed. For a young business, spreading effort across many segments can also dilute focus compared with concentrating on one strong niche.

**Judgement.** On balance, segmentation is likely to benefit FreshBox, but the size of the benefit depends on how it is done. The strongest gains — targeted, efficient marketing and a differentiated, higher-margin product with a clear USP — directly address FreshBox's current weakness of a single generic kit that appeals to no one strongly. However, as a resource-constrained start-up, FreshBox should not try to serve every segment at once. The benefits are maximised, and the added cost and complexity contained, if it initially targets ONE well-researched segment (for example, time-pressured professionals) and expands into further segments only once it has the cash flow and data to do so. Segmentation is therefore beneficial, but its value is conditional on disciplined, research-led targeting rather than fragmenting a small business's limited resources.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 10 marks:**

- **AO1 — knowledge (segmentation concepts):** the engine credits accurate command of the ideas the question turns on — what segmentation is (dividing the market into groups sharing characteristics), the bases used (demographic, behavioural, psychographic), and linked ideas such as targeting, differentiation and USP. Correct terminology signals AO1.
- **AO2 — application to THIS business:** marks are awarded for tying those concepts to FreshBox specifically — busy professionals vs families vs health-conscious cooks, advertising 15-minute kits on commuter apps, premium organic kits carrying a higher price. Generic textbook benefits that never mention the recipe-kit business do not earn AO2.
- **AO3 — balanced analysis and supported judgement:** the engine credits developed two-sided reasoning — benefits (targeted marketing, better product fit, higher sales and loyalty, spotting gaps) weighed against drawbacks (higher cost and complexity, over-segmentation, weak-research risk for a start-up) — and, crucially, a SUPPORTED JUDGEMENT that commits to a position and justifies WHY, here by conditioning the benefit on targeting one researched segment first.
- **What lifts an answer into the top band:** application plus judgement. 'Examine' expects you to consider the benefits closely AND weigh their limits, then reach a reasoned conclusion. A response that lists five benefits of segmentation but never applies them to FreshBox and never evaluates their limits is held in the lower bands, however long it is.
- **What the engine rejects:** all-benefits answers with no balance; a 'judgement' that merely restates both sides without deciding; and correct definitions with no link to FreshBox.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — AO1 for the right concepts, AO2 for applying them to the business in the question, and AO3 for balanced analysis ending in a supported judgement. So for an 'examine' or 'evaluate' question, do not just list benefits: name the concept (segmentation, targeting, positioning, USP), apply it to the specific business, weigh the benefits against the costs, and finish by committing to a judgement with a reason. The application and the judgement are where the top-band marks are won.
Worked example 2 — using a position map to justify a decision

This example shows how a position (perception) map, product differentiation and a USP come together in a recommendation. Notice again that the marks follow applied, two-sided reasoning and a supported judgement.

sections.question: ‘Lumen’ is a new independent skincare brand entering a market that already contains cheap-and-basic supermarket ranges and expensive-and-premium department-store brands. Using a position map, recommend how Lumen should position and differentiate itself. [10]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
A position map for the skincare market can be drawn with two axes customers care about: price (low to high) on one axis and perceived quality/naturalness (basic to premium-natural) on the other. Plotting the existing brands, the supermarket ranges cluster in the low-price / basic corner, while the department-store brands cluster in the high-price / premium corner. This reveals a relatively empty space: a mid-price position offering premium, natural ingredients — accessible quality — that neither cluster occupies.

**Recommended positioning.** Lumen should position itself in this gap: mid-priced skincare with a premium, natural, ethical feel — 'department-store quality without the department-store price'. This positioning is credible only if Lumen genuinely differentiates the product to match, so the perception is backed by reality.

**Differentiation and USP.** Lumen can differentiate through ingredients (natural, cruelty-free formulations), packaging (recyclable, understated premium design) and brand story (independent, transparent sourcing). Its USP could be 'clinically effective natural skincare at a mid-market price', an ethical-plus-value proposition that neither the cheap basics nor the costly premium brands offer. Because this USP is meaningful to a growing segment of value-conscious but ethically minded buyers, it can anchor the whole marketing mix — a mid-market price, promotion through beauty influencers who value authenticity, and selective stockists that feel premium but not exclusive.

**Weighing it up.** The gap on the map is attractive because it faces less direct competition and targets a growing psychographic segment. But an empty space on a map is not a guaranteed opportunity: it may be under-served precisely because customers who want premium naturalness are willing to pay premium prices, so a mid-price brand might be perceived as 'not quite premium'. There is also a real risk of being squeezed from both sides — undercut on price by supermarket ranges and out-branded on prestige by department-store names. Executing the positioning also demands consistent quality and marketing spend that a new independent brand may find hard to sustain.

**Judgement.** Lumen should pursue the mid-price, premium-natural position, because it aligns a genuine gap on the map with a meaningful USP (effective natural skincare at an accessible price) aimed at a clearly growing segment. However, the recommendation is conditional: Lumen must first validate through research that customers actually want this combination rather than assuming the map gap is real demand, and it must invest enough in product quality and brand storytelling to make the 'premium-natural' perception believable. Positioned and differentiated this way — and defended against being squeezed by a consistent, well-communicated USP — the gap represents Lumen's best route to establishing itself. A 'me-too' position next to either existing cluster would leave it competing on the terms of far larger rivals, which for a start-up is the weaker option.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 10 marks:**

- **AO1:** correct use of positioning, position/perception map, product differentiation and USP, including that a map plots perceptions on chosen attributes.
- **AO2:** application to Lumen — plotting the supermarket vs department-store clusters, identifying the mid-price / premium-natural gap, and a differentiation/USP built from natural ingredients and ethical branding.
- **AO3:** balanced analysis — the appeal of the gap weighed against the risk that the space is empty for a reason and that Lumen could be squeezed from both sides — ending in a SUPPORTED JUDGEMENT that commits to the position but conditions it on research and investment.
- **Top-band trigger:** applying the map to Lumen AND reaching a justified recommendation. A generic explanation of what a position map is, with no plotted brands and no decision, stays in the lower bands.

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Confusing segmentation, targeting and positioning** — segmentation DIVIDES the market, targeting CHOOSES a segment, positioning SHAPES perception. Describing one but naming another loses AO1 and derails the answer.

**Confusing segmentation with differentiating the product line** — 'selling a cheap and a premium version' is a product decision; segmentation divides the MARKET into customer groups. Keep the two apart.

**Listing all four bases mechanically without applying them** — a bare list (demographic, geographic, psychographic, behavioural) earns AO1 only; marks climb when you pick the bases that fit the specific business and say why.
**Treating a USP as a slogan** — the USP is the real, meaningful distinguishing feature/benefit; the slogan is the phrase that communicates it. And a difference no customer values is not a USP.

**Misreading a position map** — the axes show perceptions on chosen attributes, not sales or market share, and an empty space may be a gap OR a space customers do not want. Do not treat every gap as a guaranteed opportunity.

**Ignoring the costs of segmentation/targeting** — over-segmenting or serving too many segments raises cost and complexity, especially for a small firm; a one-sided 'segmentation is always good' answer cannot reach the top band.

**Answering 'examine'/'evaluate' with no supported judgement** — giving both sides but never committing to a justified conclusion caps the answer below the top band. Application PLUS a reasoned judgement is what wins the marks.

sections.content: Where this leads

Marketing planning is the frame that later 4.x topics fill in. The target market and positioning chosen here drive every element of the marketing mix — the 4 Ps of product, price, promotion and place — and the USP shapes branding and product decisions further on. The evaluation habit built in this lesson is the transferable skill: identify the concept (segmentation, targeting, positioning, differentiation, USP), apply it to the specific business, weigh both sides, then commit to a justified judgement. Master that template and you have the approach that earns marks across every application and evaluation question in Business Management.

sections.label: Get a Paper 2 question marked: examine or evaluate a marketing-planning decision — segmentation, targeting, positioning or a USP — applying the concepts to the business and reaching a supported judgement
